items.q: Why did the English Civil War break out in 1642?
items.correct: It arose from disputes over royal power, religion and money between Charles I and Parliament, not from a single cause.
items.misconception: The Civil War was simply about whether England should be Catholic or Protestant.
items.random_wrong: The Civil War was a foreign invasion of England.
items.why: Students reduce it to religion alone; financial, constitutional and personal grievances combined to make compromise impossible.

items.q: Did the execution of Charles I create lasting republican government?
items.correct: It produced the Commonwealth and Cromwell's Protectorate, but the monarchy was restored in 1660, showing the republic was unstable.
items.misconception: England remained a permanent republic after 1649.
items.random_wrong: Charles I's son was also executed in 1649.
items.why: Learners assume regicide ended monarchy for good; the republic collapsed within a decade and Charles II returned.

items.q: What did the Glorious Revolution of 1688 settle?
items.correct: It established that monarchs ruled with Parliament's consent, confirmed by the Bill of Rights, limiting royal power permanently.
items.misconception: The Glorious Revolution made England a republic.
items.random_wrong: It restored Catholic monarchy to England.
items.why: Students confuse limiting the crown with abolishing it; 1688 kept the monarchy but subordinated it to Parliament.

items.q: What is the period 1629-40 when Charles I ruled without Parliament called?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The Personal Rule. “The Restoration” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What happened to Charles I in 1649?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: He was tried and executed. “He fled to France” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What did the Restoration of 1660 bring back?
items.why: Correct answer: The monarchy under Charles II. “A republic” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What was the outcome of the Glorious Revolution of 1688?
items.correct: William and Mary replaced James II
items.misconception: Charles I returned to the throne
items.why: Correct answer: William and Mary replaced James II. “Charles I returned to the throne” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was Charles I executed?
items.why: The real figure is 1649 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
